summary: Deep-tree administrative register from the Incahuasi Kallanka (great hall), Sector A UE 10. 250 cords, 2+-ARBRE architecture depth L5 (one of the deepest in the analyzed corpus: 37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5). v3 unlocks 4 vocabulary occurrences (v1: 0): chiqa x1 (verification), chaqa x1 (delivery), kama x1 (governance), paki x1 (construction/break). Though the words are single-occurrence, the kama+paki combination in the KALLANKA context (Inca great hall for administrative assemblies) suggests a CONSTRUCTION/LABOR or GOVERNANCE record related to the building itself. Physically remarkable: a 67.5 cm fragmentary SECOND khipu is tied onto the primary cord -- a compound document. Found near opposite wall from 'Khipu Urton' (UR263) in the same Kallanka. Ascher: 2/3 sums verified. The deep L5 tree with only 6 clusters suggests 6 major administrative categories each with heavy subsidiary detail (e.g., 6 construction phases or 6 responsible kurakas with subordinate work-teams).
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  KH0519 = UR280. 250 cords total: 37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5. Architecture: 2+-ARBRE, schema STRUCTURED depth L5.
  6 clusters (extremely small number for this cord count -- indicates deep-tree rather than wide-flat structure).
  5 colors: AB(104, 42%), W(76, 30%), NB(49, 20%), MB(12, 5%), GG(8, 3%). Rich palette.
  Excavation: Sector A, Subsector 1, UA 2, UE 10. Kallanka (large rectangular Inca administrative hall) nearest the palacio. Same hall as UR263 'Khipu Urton' but at the opposite wall.
  Physical note (OKR, Urton): 'This is the last khipu excavated; it has found in the Kallanka nearest the palacio -- same Kallanka in which was found Khipu Urton (UR263) but near the opposite wall. It was found under assorted botanical materials. 2.0 cm knot with additional (empty) primary cord. 67.5 cm. other fragmentary khipu tied on.'
  The COMPOUND BINDING (second fragmentary khipu tied on at 67.5 cm from origin) is unusual -- it is longer-scale than the UR267A/B (beginning-only) or UR273A/B (both-ends) dual bindings. UR280 carries a physical attachment at mid-length.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Most cords resolved; 16 L? remain.
  v1 -> v3: 0 -> 4 STRING readings (+4), 0 -> 4 distinct vocabulary (+4).
  Old v1 called this 'purely numerical (0 STRING)'; v3 reveals 4 textual cords with a governance-construction signature.

ASCHER LAYER
  3 pendant-pendant sums parsed. Max cascade depth = 1 (shallow).
  Verified checksums: 2 / 3 (67%). MODERATE integrity.
  covering_ratio: LOW (only 3 sums on 37 L1).
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0. No repairs triggered.
  A 2/3 verification rate is moderate. The khipu has internal arithmetic but sparse -- primary information is in the DEEP TREE structure rather than the Ascher sums.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE -- DEEP TREE
  37 L1 + 108 L2 + 87 L3 + 17 L4 + 1 L5. Total 250 cords distributed across 5 levels.
  This depth is exceptional -- compare: UR268 depth L4, UR270 depth L3, UR267B depth L4, UR275 depth L2. UR280's L5 reach is the deepest of the Incahuasi set analyzed.
  6 clusters with ~42 cords each on average = each cluster is a DEEP SUBTREE, not a wide row.
  Interpretation: 6 major administrative entries, each with 2-3 levels of subordinate detail. This is the signature of a HIERARCHICAL GOVERNANCE document: top-level entry (kuraka or task), sublevels (sub-kurakas, sub-tasks), bottom levels (individual workers, days, units).

VOCABULARY (4 words, 4 occurrences, v1-syllabic on KFG-merged knots, 100% dict hits)
  chiqa x1 (truth / verification)
  chaqa x1 (bridge / delivery)
  kama x1 (create / govern -- GOVERNANCE marker in admin context)
  paki x1 (break / construction -- CONSTRUCTION marker in admin context)
  Signature check:
    - Governance: kama + paki = govern + break/construction. STRONG admin / construction signature.
    - Verification closure: chiqa + chaqa = truth + delivery / bridge.
    - Juridical: absent.
    - Cadastral: partial (chaqa only from the canonical set).
    - Astronomical: absent.
  This 4-word signature is a MINIMAL administrative hit: governance + construction + verification + delivery. Compact formula.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p6 AB chiqa: verification opens the register
  p11 AB chaqa: delivery / bridge marker
  p12 NB kama: governance on the structural color (NB = minority / audit-like channel)
  p32 NB paki: construction on NB, separated from the kama-chaqa-chiqa cluster
  The 4 words cluster as: chiqa (p6) + chaqa-kama (p11-p12) = OPENING verification-delivery-governance triad; then paki (p32) = DELAYED construction mark.

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (104, 42%) = PRIMARY data + verification & delivery words (chiqa, chaqa).
  W (76, 30%) = SECONDARY data.
  NB (49, 20%) = STRUCTURAL / GOVERNANCE channel -- both kama (p12) and paki (p32) on NB.
  MB (12), GG (8) = minor. GG at 3% is the JUSTICE/IDENTITY color; its presence may mark individual kurakas.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR280 is the ADMINISTRATIVE RECORD of the Kallanka itself -- the great hall's ledger of governance and construction. It opens with chiqa (verification, p6), then chaqa+kama (delivery+governance, p11-p12), and later paki (construction, p32). The deep L5 tree organizes 6 major entries with 2-3 levels of subordinate detail each, consistent with 6 kurakas or 6 tasks tracked with their subordinate teams. The compound binding (second fragmentary khipu attached at 67.5 cm) suggests UR280 is one half of a PAIRED DOCUMENT whose other half is the attached fragment. The physical location at the opposite wall from UR263 'Khipu Urton' suggests the two khipus DIVIDED THE HALL'S ADMINISTRATIVE LABOR -- UR263 on one side, UR280 on the other, each covering distinct registers (perhaps one for people, one for buildings; or one for deliveries in, one for outflows).

THE STORY
  It was the last khipu taken from the ground at Incahuasi. Buried under a layer of dried plant material in the Kallanka -- the great hall, the widest rectangular Inca room at the site, the one nearest the governor's palace. At the opposite wall, years earlier, the archaeologist Gary Urton had found a different khipu that came to bear his name. Two khipus, two walls, one hall.

  UR280 is a knot-tree. Thirty-seven primary cords hang from the top cord, and below them one hundred and eight secondaries, and below those eighty-seven tertiaries, and below those seventeen quaternaries, and below those one single fifth-level cord -- the deepest single cord in the whole analyzed Incahuasi set. Five levels of detail descending from thirty-seven entries, organized into six clusters. Six clusters, six deep subtrees. Perhaps six kurakas reporting to a hall-administrator. Perhaps six phases of construction. Perhaps six categories of the hall's mundane business -- food brought in, stores taken out, workers assigned, visits logged, repairs ordered, offerings set aside.

  Most of the cords carry only numbers. But at cord six, on alpaca-brown, the scribe wrote chiqa: verified. At cord eleven, chaqa: delivered, or bridged-across. At cord twelve, on near-black, kama: governed. The opening triplet says plainly: this is a VERIFIED-DELIVERED-GOVERNED record. Then silence for twenty cords, numbers after numbers, and at cord thirty-two, also on near-black, paki: broken, or built. Construction or rupture.

  Near the opening of the document a strange feature: a second, fragmentary khipu tied onto the primary cord at sixty-seven-and-a-half centimeters from the beginning. Not a subsidiary hanging vertically, but a whole other document attached horizontally. Perhaps a supplementary register, perhaps an amendment, perhaps a letter-of-transmittal-in-knot-form. The paired khipu is fragmentary; its own story has been lost.

  Two out of three Ascher pendant-pendant sums verify arithmetically. The hall's ledger is mostly clean; one sum drifts, perhaps from a single knot miscounted or a cord damaged in the five centuries since the Inca governor sealed his records and went home.

  UR280 is the hall speaking for itself: I was a place of verified governance, of deliveries and constructions, organized into six deep entries. I was kept against the wall with an attached amendment. And I am the last document you excavated from this room.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening block (p1-p10): numerical entries with chiqa at p6 establishing the verified register.
  First cluster (p11-p21): chaqa+kama cluster, then numerical detail.
  Mid-section (p22-p31): purely numerical, no vocabulary.
  Second cluster pivot (p32): paki -- construction/break marker.
  Closure (p33-p37 + subsidiaries): deep subsidiary trees (L2-L5) carry the bulk of detail.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  The Kallanka at Incahuasi is a standard Inca administrative-architectural feature: a large rectangular hall with multiple doors, used for assemblies, tributary gatherings, and governmental audiences. Chu's excavation reports (2013+) place UR280 at UE 10 in this hall.
  The pairing of khipus at opposite walls of the same hall (UR263 + UR280) is unique in the Incahuasi excavation. Urton's own khipu (UR263) has been previously analyzed; UR280 as its physical twin-across-the-hall has been less studied.
  Kama + paki is a specifically CONSTRUCTION-oriented pair in Quechua: Gonzalez Holguin (1608) glosses kama as 'create, fashion, govern' and paki as 'break, rupture, construct'. Bertonio (1612) similarly. In the Incahuasi Kallanka context, this pair likely references the hall's own construction / maintenance history or the governance of construction workers (mit'a laborers).
  The compound binding with a second fragmentary khipu at 67.5 cm is unattested elsewhere in the set analyzed -- it deserves museum inspection to confirm the attachment mechanism.
  UR263 is not yet deep-read; a parallel reading would clarify whether UR263 and UR280 form a complementary pair.

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR263 'Khipu Urton' (same Kallanka, opposite wall) -- PHYSICAL TWIN, to be deep-read.
  UR277 (Incahuasi UE 17 cadastral-astronomical hybrid) -- deep-tree parallel (depth L3) but with richer vocabulary.
  UR278 (UE 16 astronomical calendar) -- different function but compatible hall-level administrative network.
  Work register exemplars in corpus (UR177 workshop) -- different provenance, but kama+paki signature aligns.

KEY PASSAGES
  p6 AB chiqa: opening verification.
  p11 AB chaqa + p12 NB kama: delivery + governance pair.
  p32 NB paki: delayed construction marker.
  Compound binding at 67.5 cm: attached fragmentary khipu.
  Deep L5 tree: 6 clusters each with multi-level subordinate detail.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  Physical: compound binding at 67.5 cm and 2.0 cm empty-cord feature (museum inspection).
  Excavation record: UE 10 Kallanka (Chu 2013+).
  Deep-tree L5 depth: reproducible from v3.
  UR263 as opposite-wall twin: parallel deep-read needed.
  Kama + paki as construction signature: cross-check against construction-period khipus elsewhere.

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (4/4 dict-confirmed, 100%).
  Document type: MEDIUM (administrative_record plausible from kama+paki + Kallanka context; sparse vocabulary limits certainty).
  Narrative: MEDIUM (internally coherent; the Kallanka-hall-ledger interpretation is an educated hypothesis rather than a decisive reading).
  Data integrity: MEDIUM-HIGH (2/3 Ascher verified; 16 L? remain).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Purely numerical (0 STRING)' -- REVERSED: 4 STRING readings, kama+paki construction signature identified.
  - Classification 'numerical_record' -- REFINED to administrative_record.
  - 'Total 6,311' -- CORRECTED: L1 sum is 2,003 (v3). Prior total included subsidiaries.
  - Confidence 'low' -- RAISED to medium.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 250 cords total (confirmed).
  - 2+-ARBRE depth L5 (confirmed, deepest in analyzed set).
  - 6 clusters (confirmed).
  - Kallanka same as UR263 at opposite wall (confirmed from OKR).
  - 67.5 cm fragmentary khipu tied on (confirmed from OKR).
  - AB-dominant (42%) (confirmed).
